difficulty_factors: Polyester is oleophilic — it attracts and holds oil stains more tenaciously than natural fibers
Heat permanently sets oil on polyester — CRITICAL: treat BEFORE any heat/dryer exposure
Standard laundry often fails because polyester's hydrophobic surface resists water-based cleaning

diy.title: Cooking Oil on Polyester
ingredients: Warm water (NOT hot — polyester heat-sets oil)
mixSteps: No mixing needed — apply Dawn directly to the dry stain
Use warm water only (not hot) — polyester heat-sets oil at lower temperatures than cotton
Have cornstarch ready for absorption step

steps.instruction: Blot excess oil. DO NOT put in the dryer — this is the single most important rule for oil on polyester.
steps.why: Polyester heat-sets oil. The dryer turns a removable stain into a permanent one. This is not recoverable.

steps.instruction: Apply cornstarch or baking soda to the stain. Wait 15-20 minutes.
steps.why: Absorbs surface oil. Polyester is oleophilic (oil-loving), so the oil bonds strongly — powder helps break initial adhesion.

steps.instruction: Brush off powder. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the DRY stain. Work in with a brush.
steps.why: Polyester's oleophilic surface requires concentrated surfactant contact. Soap on dry fabric = maximum oil emulsification.

steps.instruction: Let the soap sit for 10-15 minutes — longer dwell time than cotton because polyester holds oil more tightly.
steps.why: Polyester's hydrophobic affinity for oil means the surfactant needs extra time to penetrate and encapsulate the oil droplets.

steps.instruction: Rinse with warm water (not hot). Wash in machine on warm with extra detergent.
steps.why: Warm water assists emulsification without the heat-setting risk. Extra detergent compensates for polyester's oil-holding tendency.

steps.instruction: CHECK the stain before drying. If any shadow remains, repeat from step 3. Air dry until clear.
steps.why: This step saves the garment. One dryer cycle on a residual oil stain = permanent.

diy.ifStillThere: Apply Lestoil Heavy Duty Cleaner directly, soak 30 minutes, wash warm. For persistent stains, try Carbona Stain Devils #5 (solvent-based — more effective than surfactant on polyester). If heat-set, a professional dry cleaner with POG solvents may be able to partially recover — but honestly, heat-set oil on polyester is the hardest stain to recover in the industry.
proTips: The dryer is the enemy. Repeat after me: check the stain BEFORE drying.
Polyester needs more aggressive degreasing than cotton because it's oleophilic — oil bonds to polyester like a magnet.
Dawn applied to DRY fabric is still the #1 home method, but expect to need 2-3 rounds on polyester.
diy.safety: Polyester tolerates most cleaning agents. It's chemically resistant to acids, alkalis, and most solvents. The only enemy is heat + oil.
diy.whyThisWorks: Polyester (PET polymer) has a hydrophobic, oleophilic surface — it literally attracts oil molecules through van der Waals forces. Standard water-based washing struggles because water beads off polyester's surface. Concentrated dish soap applied directly to the dry stain bypasses this: the surfactant's oil-loving end bonds directly to the oil, forming micelles that make it water-soluble. The key is contact time — polyester releases oil slowly.

home.title: Cooking Oil on Polyester — Treat Before Heat
home.confidenceNote: High success if treated BEFORE heat exposure. Heat-set oil on polyester is extremely difficult to recover.

primaryProduct.why: Concentrated surfactant applied directly to dry stain — best approach for polyester's oleophilic surface

alternativeProducts.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)

alternativeProducts.why: Petroleum solvent base for stubborn oil on polyester

home.prepStep: DO NOT PUT IN DRYER. Blot excess oil. Apply cornstarch for 15-20 minutes before wet treatment.

method.instruction: DO NOT DRY. Blot excess oil. Apply cornstarch for 15-20 minutes. Brush off.
method.why: Absorbs surface oil. Every minute before treatment counts on polyester's oleophilic surface.

method.instruction: Apply Dawn Platinum directly to the DRY stain. Work in gently. Let sit 10-15 minutes.
method.why: Longer dwell than cotton — polyester releases oil slowly. Concentrated surfactant contact is essential.

method.instruction: Rinse with warm water (not hot). Repeat the Dawn application if stain is still visible.
method.why: Warm water assists without heat-setting. Multiple rounds often needed on polyester.

method.instruction: Wash in machine on warm with extra detergent.
method.why: Extra detergent compensates for polyester's oil affinity.

method.instruction: INSPECT before drying. If shadow remains, repeat treatment. AIR DRY until clear.
method.why: Heat-setting oil on polyester is essentially permanent. Always check before the dryer.

materialWarnings.warning: HEAT SETS OIL ON POLYESTER PERMANENTLY. Do NOT use dryer, iron, or hot water until stain is completely gone. This is the #1 rule.
Polyester is oleophilic — expect to need 2-3 treatment rounds. One pass of soap rarely gets all the oil.
Warm water only — hot water risks heat-setting. Polyester's heat tolerance is high for clean fabric, but oil changes the equation.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: If Carbona doesn't work, try Lestoil Heavy Duty Cleaner (petroleum solvent). If all home methods fail, a professional dry cleaner with POG solvents can dissolve oil that surfactants can't emulsify on polyester's oleophilic surface.

proTips: Check before drying. Check before drying. Check before drying.
Apply Dawn to DRY stain — never wet polyester oil stains first.
Solvent-based products (Carbona #5) often work better than surfactant alone on polyester because polyester's oleophilic surface resists water-based approaches.
home.whyThisWorks: Polyester's PET polymer surface attracts oil through hydrophobic bonding. Concentrated surfactant applied dry overcomes this affinity. The critical factor isn't chemistry — it's avoiding heat.

pro.title: Cooking Oil on Polyester — Professional Protocol (Pre-Spot Before Heat)
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (lipid-based triglyceride). Cooking oils are triglycerides of C16-C18 fatty acids. On polyester (PET polymer — oleophilic, hydrophobic, heat tolerance 140°C for clean fabric), oil bonds strongly through van der Waals forces between the nonpolar oil molecules and polyester's nonpolar aromatic ring structure. Polyester literally attracts and holds oil — this is why polyester clothing develops that dingy, greasy feel over time. The critical issue: heat-setting. When oil-contaminated polyester is heated (dryer, pressing, even hot wash), the oil migrates into the amorphous regions of the PET polymer and becomes trapped as the polymer re-crystallizes during cooling. This is effectively permanent. The professional imperative: POG pre-spot BEFORE any heat in the cleaning cycle.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG or Street's POG
spottingProtocol.technique: Aggressive dry-side POG pre-spot — dissolve oil before any heat exposure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG generously to the oil stain. Polyester requires more aggressive POG treatment than natural fibers because of its oleophilic affinity. Use the bone spatula with firm tamping — polyester's synthetic structure tolerates mechanical action well. You'll need more rounds than on cotton or silk: 3-4 applications with fresh towel positions. The oil releases in stages from polyester's surface. Watch the towel — when transfer slows to minimal shadow, POG has done its job. Dan Eisen's principle of pre-spotting before the cleaning cycle is CRITICAL on polyester. The dry cleaning heat, even moderate, will set any residual oil.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent flush to remove dissolved oil
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with VDS to carry dissolved oil through the fabric. Feather edges. Apply compressed air to dry. Polyester is resistant to solvent ring marks, but feathering is still professional practice.

spottingProtocol.agent: Machine cleaning — PERC or HC solvent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Both PERC and HC are rated A (excellent) for polyester. PERC has higher solvency for oil. HC is gentler. GreenEarth also excellent. The POG pre-spot addressed the concentrated stain — the machine cycle handles residual oil uniformly. Monitor drying temperature — even in the dry cleaning machine, excessive heat can set residual oil on polyester.

spottingProtocol.technique: Visual check under transmitted and reflected light
spottingProtocol.instruction: Inspect under both transmitted light (oil shows as translucent area) and reflected light (oil shows as darker spot). Polyester hides oil stains when freshly solvent-cleaned — they can reappear as the fabric reaches thermal equilibrium. Wait until fully room temperature before final inspection. If any oil residue detected, repeat POG and re-clean BEFORE pressing. Heat from the press will permanently set any residual oil.

pro.escalation: For heat-set oil on polyester: this is one of the most difficult stain recoveries in the industry. Repeat aggressive POG with extended dwell (5+ minutes). Try amyl acetate. D-limonene may partially dissolve polymerized oil in the amorphous PET regions. Multiple cleaning cycles may be needed. Be honest with the customer: heat-set oil on polyester has limited recovery prospects.
pro.customerExplanation: Polyester actually attracts and holds cooking oil — it's one of the more challenging combinations in fabric care. We pre-treated the oil with a professional degreasing solvent before the cleaning cycle, ensuring the oil was dissolved before any heat touched the fabric. That pre-treatment step is what makes the difference.
commonMistakes: Any heat before oil is fully removed — the #1 irreversible mistake on polyester. This includes: dryer, pressing iron, hot water wash, even a warm dryer cycle.
Assuming one round of POG is enough — polyester's oleophilic surface releases oil in stages. 3-4 rounds minimum.
Using only water-based cleaning without solvent pre-spot — water-based detergent alone cannot overcome polyester's hydrophobic oil bonding.
Hot pressing before inspection — hides oil temporarily but sets it permanently.
pro.neutralization: No acid neutralization needed for dry-side protocol. If wet-side was used, standard acetic acid rinse.
pro.fabricRisks: Polyester is chemically resistant to most agents. The primary risk is heat-setting oil, not chemical damage. Static buildup after cleaning — use anti-static treatment.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson NovaBone POG remover professional spotting
professional.note: Primary POG agent. CRITICAL: pre-spot polyester BEFORE any heat in the cleaning cycle.

professional.name: Street's POG Remover
professional.note: Industry standard. Use aggressively on polyester — 3-4 rounds.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Formula 83 VDS
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson Formula 83 volatile dry solvent
professional.note: For flushing POG and feathering edges after pre-spot treatment.

household.name: Dawn Platinum Dish Soap
household.note: Apply to DRY stain. Expect 2-3 rounds on polyester. Longer dwell time than cotton.

household.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)
household.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils fat cooking oil
household.note: Solvent-based — often more effective than surfactant alone on polyester's oleophilic surface.

household.name: Lestoil Heavy Duty Cleaner
household.note: Strongest consumer degreaser. Apply, soak 30 min, wash warm.

title: Cooking Oil on Polyester
stainChemistry: Cooking oil is a triglyceride that bonds tenaciously to polyester through hydrophobic affinity. Polyester (PET polymer) is oleophilic — its aromatic ring structure creates strong van der Waals interactions with nonpolar oil molecules. This is why polyester clothing 'grays' over time — it accumulates body oil that standard laundry can't fully remove. The critical danger: heat-setting. When oil-contaminated polyester is heated, the oil migrates into the amorphous polymer regions and becomes trapped during re-crystallization. This is irreversible at the molecular level.
whyThisWorks: POG solvent overcomes polyester's oleophilic bonding through like-dissolves-like solvency — the solvent molecules compete with polyester's surface for the oil molecules and win. Multiple rounds are needed because polyester releases oil in stages. The dry-side approach prevents any heat exposure until oil is confirmed removed.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board, clean towels, bone spatula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG generously. Firm tamping — polyester handles it. Multiple rounds with fresh towel positions. This step is non-negotiable on polyester.
Flush with VDS. Feather edges. Dry with compressed air.
All solvents rated A for polyester. Monitor drying temperature — even in the machine.
Wait until room temperature. Check under both transmitted and reflected light. Oil can reappear on polyester. NEVER press until oil is confirmed gone.

homeSolutions: Dawn on dry stain: Apply directly to DRY oil stain. Work in. Let sit 10-15 minutes. Wash WARM (not hot). Expect 2-3 rounds on polyester.
Cornstarch + Dawn: Powder first (15-20 min), then Dawn direct. Gives the best results on polyester.
Carbona Stain Devils #5: Solvent-based — often outperforms surfactant alone on polyester because polyester's surface is oleophilic.
Lestoil soak: For stubborn oil, apply Lestoil and soak 30 minutes. Wash warm.
NEVER use the dryer until the stain is confirmed gone. This is the single most important instruction for polyester.
materialWarnings: HEAT SETS OIL ON POLYESTER PERMANENTLY. No dryer, no iron, no hot water until oil stain is completely gone.
Polyester is oleophilic — expect to need 2-3 treatment rounds. One application rarely gets all the oil.
Use warm water, not hot. Even warm water at the high end can begin heat-setting oil on polyester.
Check the stain after EVERY wash cycle before drying. Oil stains on polyester look lighter when wet.
commonMistakes: The dryer — putting oil-stained polyester in the dryer is the #1 irreversible mistake. Heat-set oil on polyester is essentially permanent.
One round of soap — polyester releases oil in stages. Multiple treatments are the norm, not the exception.
Hot water — cotton handles hot water for oil, but polyester's heat-setting risk makes warm the safer choice.
Assuming the stain is gone when wet — oil stains on polyester look lighter when fabric is wet with soap solution. Always inspect when dry.
scienceNote: Polyester (polyethylene terephthalate, PET) has aromatic rings in its polymer backbone that create a hydrophobic, oleophilic surface. Oil molecules bond through van der Waals forces (London dispersion) between the nonpolar oil chains and polyester's aromatic surface. Heat-setting occurs because at elevated temperatures, the amorphous regions of PET become mobile enough for oil molecules to penetrate. When the polymer re-crystallizes during cooling, the oil is physically trapped within the crystal structure — this is irreversible short of dissolving the polymer itself. This is the same chemistry that makes polyester hard to dye — and hard to un-stain.
customerExplanation: Polyester actually attracts cooking oil — the fabric's chemistry creates a natural affinity for grease. The key to removal was treating with a professional solvent BEFORE any heat touched the fabric. Once oil is heat-set into polyester, it's extremely difficult to recover. We caught it in time.
escalation: Heat-set oil on polyester: aggressive POG with extended dwell + amyl acetate + d-limonene. Multiple cleaning cycles. Be transparent with customer about limited recovery prospects for heat-set oil on polyester.
callPro: Call a professional when: the polyester garment has been through the dryer with oil still present; you've done 3+ rounds of home treatment without success; the stain is aged and shows yellowing. Professional POG solvents are significantly stronger than consumer products for polyester's oleophilic surface.
solventNote: POG pre-spot on dry side BEFORE any heat in the cleaning cycle. This is the single most important instruction for oil on polyester. All dry cleaning solvents rated A for polyester.
